title: The Pediatrician Who Made You Feel Crazy
dek: You weren’t an anxious mother. You weren’t hysterical. The pediatrician who dismissed your child’s symptoms trained you to doubt your own perception, and the anger that followed wasn’t a breakdown  -  it was your nervous system waking up. This is what happens when medical authority overwrites your body’s knowing, and why reclaiming that fury is the first honest breath you’ve taken in years.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. The Pediatrician Who Made You Feel Crazy is a place where the body and the spirit meet, and it asks for honest attention rather than control. In our experience, healthy anger is information about a boundary, a value, or a longing that has been crossed or unmet. This piece walks you through what to notice, what to try, and what to leave alone, in plain language and with real care.

The fury that flooded your chest when that pediatrician said, “some kids are just dramatic,” was not a sign of your irrationality. It was the first clean signal your body had sent in months, after you’d overridden every whisper of its knowing. You didn’t lose your mind in that exam room. You found the edge of a self that had been trained into silence. The real crazy-making wasn’t your anger - it was the entire performance that came before it, the one where you smiled and nodded while your gut screamed. *Last updated May 18, 2026.* ## The Waiting Room That Taught You to Doubt The body remembers the smell. Rubbing alcohol and the faintly sweet plastic of well-washed toys, the kind that never quite dry. Your son’s weight on your lap, his warmth seeping through your jeans, his cough a wet rattle against your sternum. You’d been here three times in eight weeks. Three co-pays, three times undressing a feverish toddler on a crinkly paper sheet, three times hearing that it was “just a virus” while a voice inside you whispered something sharper. The pediatrician’s office was not a place of healing. It was an academy of self-doubt, and you were the star pupil. You watched other parents flip through *Highlights* magazines with an ease that felt foreign. They accepted the script. Their babies had runny noses, needed a signature for daycare, and they left. But you - you had started to feel a low-grade hum beneath your skin, a vibration that wouldn’t settle. Your jaw stayed tight from the moment you signed in at the front desk. By the time the nurse called your son’s name, your shoulders had climbed an inch toward your ears, as if bracing for a blow. That posture was not anxiety. It was the physical shape of a woman being gaslit, the body’s way of preparing to absorb another round of dismissal. The longer you sat, the more your own senses blurred. Was the rash on his belly truly spreading, or were you hallucinating? Was his lethargy a toddler’s normal post-fever slump, or the kind of listlessness that keeps a nurse on a hospital floor up at night? The waiting room didn’t just ask you to wait. It asked you to abandon the evidence of your own eyes in exchange for a laminated sheet of sick-child protocols. Your body, though, never stopped tracking. The body remembers the quick dart of your gaze to the clock, the clench of your thighs every time his breathing hitched. It stored every detail while your mind agreed to amnesia. ## The Moment Your Perception Was Stolen “Some kids are just dramatic.” The pediatrician said it without looking up from his tablet, tapping the screen with a stylus while your son stood shirtless, his ribs pressing against skin that looked too pale. You had just explained, for the fourth time, that he wasn’t eating, that his sleep had shattered, that the night before you’d counted his respirations at forty breaths a minute while he lay limp in your bed. Your voice was steady. You’d rehearsed the facts in the car. But the doctor’s words landed in the room like a sharp stone dropped into still water, and everything inside you went silent. You felt the heat first. It rose from your belly, up through your sternum, and lodged in your throat - a physical plug of rage that you swallowed back. Your fingers curled around the edge of the exam table, the paper crinkling in your grip. You didn’t scream. You didn’t argue. You did what you were trained to do: you smiled, a thin, apologetic curve that made you look reasonable, and you said, “Okay. I’m probably overthinking it.” That sentence was a betrayal, and your body knew it. The moment the words left your mouth, a cold numbness spread across the back of your neck, a freeze response so familiar you’d mistaken it for your actual personality. Psychotherapist and author of *Rage Becomes Her*, Soraya Chemaly, points out that this self-silencing isn’t a character flaw - it’s a social mandate, especially for women. She writes that anger in women is routinely pathologized, reframed as hysteria or anxiety, so that the status quo remains unchallenged. In that exam room, you were not “anxious.” You were furious. But the pediatrician’s authority, coupled with a culture that brands maternal anger as dangerous, made you convert it into self-blame on the spot. The body, however, doesn’t accept these conversions cleanly. The anger didn’t disappear; it went underground, into the tight muscles of your pelvic floor, the shallowness of your breath, the migraines that would become a weekly ritual. ## What Peter Levine Would Say About Your Frozen Smile The smile you gave that pediatrician is what trauma researcher Peter Levine calls a “trauma-made gesture” - an incomplete self-protective response that the body holds in suspension. In his work on somatic experiencing, Levine describes how animals in the wild shake off threat after a near-miss, discharging the surge of adrenaline. Humans, trained by politeness and hierarchy, clamp down instead. Your smile, that small, agreeable baring of teeth, was a survival strategy that never got resolved. The body remembers the urge to bare your teeth in a completely different way - to snarl, to push back, to say, “You are not listening to me” - and it holds that tension in the jaw, the temporalis muscle, the occiput, until you give it another option. For weeks after that appointment, you’d wake with a sore jaw. Your dentist would suggest a night guard, as if the problem were dental. But the grinding wasn’t about malocclusion. It was the muffled grinding down of a protest you weren’t allowed to voice. Levine’s framework says that trauma isn’t the event itself; it’s the undischarged activation stuck in the nervous system. The pediatrician didn’t traumatize you in that moment. The trauma was your own organism’s desperate, thwarted attempt to protect your child, strangled by a lifetime of conditioning to be “nice.” Each night, your jaw tried to finish the sentence your mouth couldn’t. Somatic work often begins with noticing these small, stalled actions. In the parking lot of the medical building, you could have put the car in park, placed both hands on the steering wheel, and let the trembling start. But you didn’t. You drove home, went through the bedtime routine, and the undischarged fury settled deeper. This is the cost of being a “good” patient, a “reasonable” mother. The bill comes due in the body’s quiet, chronic contractions. ## The Anger You Mistook for Anxiety By the third month of your son’s illness, you had a diagnosis for yourself: generalized anxiety disorder. Your GP listened to your litany - racing heart, insomnia, a constant hum of dread - and wrote a prescription without ever asking what had happened before the symptoms began. It felt orderly, this label. It gave you a little bottle of medication and a pathology that was yours alone. Anxiety, after all, is an internal fault. It lives in your chemistry, your rumination, your faulty amygdala. Anger, on the other hand, is relational. It points outward. It says someone did something wrong. That was much harder to swallow. The racing heart wasn’t a panic attack. It was your sinoatrial node responding to a suppressed impulse to fight. That dread wasn’t free-floating; it had a very specific address. You dreaded the next appointment, the next phone call with the nurse who said “the doctor isn’t concerned,” the next night of watching your child’s chest rise and fall while your own lungs felt caged. The anxiety label was a trick mirror. It turned a reasonable, self-protective fury into a private shame. You started to think, “I’m the crazy one. I’m the problem.” In the angriest moments, you’d even feel a flash of heat and then immediate guilt, apologizing to a husband who hadn’t been in the room, to a God you didn’t believe in, for being too much. Robert Augustus Masters, a psychotherapist who has spent decades working with the raw territory of rage, writes that anger is not the enemy; it is a “fierce ally,” a guardian of boundaries and a clear-eyed messenger. His book *Knowing Your Shadow* insists that the real damage comes not from anger itself, but from the shame we wrap around it. When you called yourself anxious, you were deepening a wound that only honesty could heal. The body, though, was still on your side. It kept giving you signals: a sudden flush when you drove past the medical building, a tightness in your throat when you saw the pediatrician’s name on caller ID. These weren’t malfunctions. They were flares in the dark, and you were alive enough to feel them. ## The Shame of Being “That Mother” You knew what the receptionist thought. You could see it in the way she said, “Oh, Mrs. ____, you’re back again,” her voice a thin veneer of professionalism over a yawn. You became “that mother” - the one who Googled too much, the one who took up appointment slots with “worried well” complaints, the one whose child was probably just a picky eater with seasonal allergies. The shame was a physical weight in your belly, a heavy stone that made you sit up straighter and speak in a higher register, trying to sound less demanding. You began sentences with apologies: “I’m sorry to take up your time, but…” You laughed small, self-effacing laughs at the end of your concerns, to signal you didn’t really believe them yourself. This shame is not a personal weakness. Soraya Chemaly’s work documents how institutions uphold this dynamic: women’s reports of pain and symptoms are systematically taken less seriously than men’s, and mothers fall into a particularly dismissible category. The shaming is a mechanism of control. If you feel insane, you’ll stop advocating. If you stop advocating, the system doesn’t have to change. The pediatrician’s ease was purchased with your silence. Even your partner, well-meaning, would say, “Maybe he’s right, maybe it’s just a phase.” And you’d feel the familiar slide into isolation, the loneliness of a body that knew the truth but couldn’t convince anyone else. The body remembers lying in bed next to a sleeping spouse, your hand on your own sternum, pressing down to feel the solid beat of your heart - a primitive reassurance that you were still here, still sensing, still sane. ## A Five-Minute Practice for the Driveway You don’t need a dark room and forty minutes of meditation. You need something you can use right after the next appointment, or after the next phone call that makes you small. When you get back to your car, don’t start the engine. Sit. Place both hands on the steering wheel or on your thighs, and press your feet firmly into the floor mat. Feel the solid resistance against your soles. Notice where your body is making contact - the seat on the backs of your legs, the small of your back against the lumbar support. Then, with your eyes open or closed, find a place in your body that holds heat or tension. It might be your jaw. It might be the pit of your stomach. It might be a band around your ribcage. Once you’ve found it, give it a tiny, deliberate movement. If your jaw is tight, let your teeth unclench just a millimeter and then clench again, very slowly, as if you’re chewing the words you wanted to say. If your hands are clenched, push them into your thighs and then release, letting the tremble come if it wants to. Stay with the sensation for two minutes. Then, silently or aloud, say one true thing: “I am angry because my child was not seen.” Or, “I am angry because I was told to doubt myself.” No apology. No “I know it’s silly, but…” Just the statement, paired with a full exhale. Let the exhale be a little longer than the inhale. When the impulse to move rises, honor it: shake your hands out, stamp your feet on the floor of the car, make a low sound from the back of your throat. Then drive away. This is not a cure. This is a reclamation of the body’s right to complete the protective response that was frozen. In the weeks that followed, you might find that your nervous system needs more support than a single practice can offer. Some people reach for botanical allies when sleep stays frayed. A friend might press a bottle of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20) into your hand, not as a cure for rage, but as a grounding anchor for a body that had been on high alert for months. You’d take it dutifully, noticing after a week that the morning dread arrived later and later, that your shoulders were no longer set in concrete by breakfast. ## The Second Visit: When You Finally Said No The day you switched pediatricians, your hands didn’t shake. A new receptionist, a different building, an intake form that asked, “What are your chief concerns?” in a blank box, giving you room. Your son, now thinner, with dark circles under his eyes, was quiet in the stroller. This new doctor, a woman with gray-streaked hair and a swift directness, examined him without a tablet in sight. She listened to your story without interrupting. When you got to the part about the previous doctor, she met your eyes and said, “You knew something was wrong. What did you see?” That question, so simple, undid something in your chest. You listed the symptoms - the weight loss, the night sweats, the sporadic low fevers - and she nodded, not to agree, but to match your pace, to follow your thread. You didn’t cry, though the pressure in your throat threatened. Instead, you felt your shoulders drop, a genuine drop, the kind that comes when a threat is finally removed. The body remembers the exact angle of your spine as it settled, the new space between your vertebrae. For the first time in six months, your breath reached your belly. The diagnosis came later: a walking pneumonia that had been festering, a subtle immune dysregulation that needed attention. It wasn’t catastrophic, but it wasn’t “just a virus.” Your anger had been the correct diagnostic tool all along. The rage that you’d bottled up wasn’t a neurosis; it was the precise signal that something in your child’s care was negligent. The fury was never about the virus. It was about the dismissal, the systematic grinding down of your trust in your own senses. Chemaly writes that anger is “the demand for change,” the recognition that an injustice has occurred and must be named. You had finally named it, not by shouting at the old doctor, but by walking out and finding someone who would look. Later, when a holistic practitioner listened to your story, she pressed a small bottle of [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) into your palm. “For the stagnation,” she said. The name meant “Free and Easy Wanderer” - a bitter irony that you savored. For weeks, you’d been anything but free. You took the teapills as directed, and while you didn’t expect herbs to undo the betrayal, you noticed that the tightness in your ribcage began to loosen, the kind of release that comes when the body understands it’s no longer in a fight it cannot win. ## The Questions You Google at 2 AM **Is it normal to still be angry months later?** Yes. Anger has a half-life that doesn’t follow a calendar. The body doesn’t process a betrayal on a timeline. The part of you that’s still furious isn’t broken - it’s vigilant. The key is not to eliminate the anger, but to stop shaming yourself for its presence. When it rises, ask it: “What do you still need me to know?” The answer is rarely about the old pediatrician; it’s usually about a boundary you haven’t set yet in your present life. **How do I deal with family members who think I’m overreacting?** You don’t convince them. That’s a trap. Your body’s knowing doesn’t require a vote. You can say, “I’m not looking for agreement right now; I’m following what I know,” and let the silence sit. The more you defend, the more you hand over your authority. Keep your focus on the child in front of you, not the audience. Your nervous system calms when you stop auditioning for approval from people who weren’t in the exam room. **What if the pediatrician was right and I was being dramatic?** Then you’d still have a body that learned you’ll abandon it the moment an authority figure frowns. The deeper issue isn’t whether your child had a serious illness; it’s that the dismissal triggered a rupture in your own self-trust. Even if the doctor were right on paper, the way it was handled taught you something toxic - that your observations don’t matter. Reclaiming anger isn’t about being right every time. It’s about insisting that your sensory, felt experience is always a legitimate starting point for any conversation about your child. Sleeplessness often tags along with this particular flavor of fury, the kind that reels through the night’s quiet hours. Some find that a formula like [Gaia Herbs SleepThru (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004GJYTF8?tag=spankyspinola-20) - used traditionally for a restless, agitated mind - helps the body drop into restoration, not as a sedative to crush the anger, but as a key to unlock the rest that anger’s hyper-vigilance had been withholding. ## What Opens When You Stop Apologizing The pediatrician was never the real target. He was a placeholder, a stand-in for every system that taught you to silence your inner alarm. The real shift happened not when you got a new doctor, but when you stopped performing the role of the easygoing mother, the one who preemptively forgives before she’s even been heard. This is the counterintuitive gift of that fury: it reacquaints you with the back of your own teeth, with the solid, unbudging “no” that lives in your perineum and diaphragm. Once you’ve felt it, it becomes impossible to unfeel. You start to notice it everywhere - the workplace meeting where you smile instead of asking for a fair deadline, the family dinner where you laugh off a comment that stung, the spousal conversation where you say “it’s fine” when your chest is a hot coal. The body’s memory is long. It has carried every unsaid thing. Now, each time you refuse to take a breath that is too shallow, each time you let the anger surface just enough to say “I hear you, but I don’t agree,” you are undoing the exam room. You are rewiring the freeze into a current of clear, present power. This isn’t about the pediatrician anymore. It’s about a life where your signaling system - your gut, your clenched jaw, your sudden flush of heat - is treated as a primary source, not a footnote. This is the underbelly of maternal love they don’t put on parenting blogs: the ferocity that looks, to the outside, like a tantrum. But inside it, you’ve discovered a truth that no placard in a clinic can teach. The anger is not the problem. The anger is the proof that you are still capable of protecting what matters. And that capacity, once honored, doesn’t just heal your child’s pneumonia or fix your sleep. It remakes the landscape of your own life, one unapologetic exhale at a time. [If you’re just starting to untangle this knot, you might need companion words that speak the same language](https://theoraclelover.com) - a place where intuition and outrage get to share a seat at the table. ## What the research and the body both say In our experience, the most useful sources on this stay close to the body. For a careful clinical view, see the NIH on anger and the autonomic nervous system. If you want to keep going inside this journal, you can read [the about page](/about), take a quiet hour with [more articles in this journal](/articles), or stay in the writing through [our herbal cabinet](/herbs). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 18, 2026. In our work on the pediatrician who made you feel crazy, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
